figures.caption: Table 1: Comparison of continual learning strategies on the real-world VLA task stream. Scores are evaluated after training on the final task and reported for all four tasks. Avg. reports the mean normalized score over the four tasks. Avg. NBT measures the average degradation on previously learned tasks, with lower value indicating better retention. Avg. FT measures the average gain or loss over single-task training when learning tasks sequentially, with higher values indicating stronger positive transfer to newly learned tasks. The results show that naive sequential fine-tuning catastrophically forgets earlier skills, whereas the default replay configuration (B = 0.2, fr = 0.2) nearly eliminates forgetting and achieves the strongest average performance, even surpassing joint training.=

Figure 1: Overview of our investigation into real-world continual VLA learning. We collect a real-world sequential manipulation dataset of four heterogeneous tasks and study whether VLA models can adapt to them sequentially without forgetting. The top panels illustrate the continual learning problem, the real-world task stream, and the training procedure. The bottom panels summarize our central findings: (1) naive sequential fine-tuning causes severe catastrophic forgetting; (2) a modest replay budget largely mitigates this forgetting; and (3) implementation-level factors—replay frequency, buffer size, and action normalization—can determine whether continual learning succeeds or collapses entirely.

Figure 3: Forgetting matrices under sequential fine-tuning. Without ER (left), all previously learned tasks collapse to near-zero performance, confirming severe catastrophic forgetting. With appropriately configured ER (right panels), forgetting is largely eliminated across all tasks. However, excessively high replay frequency impairs new-task learning, while insufficient replay data weakens retention—revealing a U-shaped sensitivity that governs the stability–plasticity trade-off.

Figure 4: Replay effectiveness exhibits a U-shaped sensitivity to buffer size and replay frequency. Overly frequent replay (fr = 0.5) impairs new-task acquisition, particularly for fragile tasks such as the press button task. Insufficient replay frequency (fr = 0.05) or buffer capacity (B = 0.002) weakens retention on tasks that require diverse replay trajectories, such as the hang cup task. The optimal point (B = 0.2, fr = 0.2) balances stability and plasticity, achieving strong performance across all four tasks.

Figure 2: Overview of the (a) robot platform (the left figure) and (b) task sequence (the right figure). We adopt a multi-view teleoperation platform to collect demonstrations across four diverse manipulation tasks. The four tasks span rigid-object pick-and-place, cup hanging, contact-rich pressing, and deformable-object folding. This design provides a deployment-realistic task stream for evaluating whether VLA models can learn sequentially without forgetting.
